author: mbostock

body:
This looks really amazing so far!

I noticed the "What is D3?" section is still TODO. I thought to myself wow, that would be a tough one to write, so I plugged in "What is D3.js?" to ChatGPT just out of curiosity to see what it would come up with. I was surprised at how good the response was that I'd like to share it here as potential inspiration for that section.

Prompt: What is D3.js?

Response:

D3.js, short for Data-Driven Documents, is a powerful JavaScript library used for creating interactive and dynamic data visualizations in web browsers. It provides a wide range of tools and functionality to manipulate data and generate visual representations in the form of charts, graphs, maps, and more.

D3.js is unique in its approach because it focuses on binding data to the Document Object Model (DOM), which is the structure that represents the elements of a web page. By leveraging the power of SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics), D3.js enables the creation of visually appealing and customizable visualizations that respond to changes in data.

Key features of D3.js include:

1. Data Binding: D3.js allows you to associate data with elements in the DOM, creating a connection between data values and visual representations.

2. Data Transformation: D3.js provides a variety of data manipulation methods, such as filtering, sorting, aggregating, and transforming data, which can be used to prepare data for visualization.

3. Selections and Manipulation: D3.js employs a selection API that allows you to select elements in the DOM and manipulate their attributes, styles, and content dynamically.

4. Transition and Animation: D3.js facilitates the creation of smooth transitions and animations, enabling you to bring visualizations to life and enhance user experience.

5. Scalability and Flexibility: D3.js is highly flexible and can be used to build a wide range of visualizations, from simple charts to complex interactive dashboards. It provides a low-level, declarative approach, giving developers fine-grained control over the visual representation of data.

6. Community and Ecosystem: D3.js has a large and active community of developers who contribute to its ongoing development. Additionally, there are numerous reusable D3.js modules and plugins available, making it easier to implement complex visualizations.

Overall, D3.js is a popular choice for web developers and data scientists who want to create data visualizations that are both aesthetically pleasing and interactive, leveraging the power of modern web technologies.
author: curran

body:
I rewrote the ExampleChord component to use more SSR (Vue). SSR is generally good since there are “fewer moving parts”, but it does make it harder to keep the component code in sync with the linked notebook, so I don’t think it’s hard rule that we should always SSR as much as possible. (Also in some cases the SSR’d output can be too large, as with maps.)

In any case, it would be better if this example linked to a notebook with the same Circos hair color dataset. We should probably fork the current chord diagram notebook because it’s nice to have multiple examples with different datasets.
author: mbostock

body: Oh, and I dropped the brighter/darker in favor of using the background color for stroking the ribbons.

author: Fil

body:
I think I would rather show a variety of (3–4?) static axes here—say a time scale, a linear scale, a log scale, an ordinal scale—like we do in the Plot [scales documentation](https://observablehq.com/plot/features/scales#continuous-scales), so that you can quickly see a few different forms of axes at a glance.

Setting tickSizeOuter to zero is also a little surprising since it’s not the default behavior of the axis component. (I’d rather d3-axis didn’t render the domain line by default, and didn’t have outer ticks by default even when it did, but I dunno if we should consider that a breaking change, and at any rate I’m not especially motivated to fix it since we have Plot.)

Maybe we could do a quick transition on pointerenter/pointerleave between two different domains so that we can also use the “static” axes to demonstrate transitions. Or I guess we could do a quick transition on page load; that would be more discoverable and acceptable if it’s ≤750ms.
author: mbostock

body: Maybe I'll add animation later, but we must first get the story straight :)
author: Fil

body: Given that Plot doesn’t use d3-axis, and that we’re planning on changing Plot to adopt a multi-line tick format for time axes, I think we should write this using D3 to make sure it’s representative of D3’s behavior. I’ll do that now.

body:
We removed the arrow because all the links we have planned to put in the top right take you somewhere else, as do the links in the "Made by Observable" popup. It felt odd to have arrows on all of them, and odd to have an arrow only on the version number.

@mbostock could you share your thoughts on why we'd only have an arrow on that one link, please? And will we also have one next to "observablehq.com" on https://observablehq.com/documentation/ ?
author: dunstan

body: Normally all the links off-site (that open in a new window) have a little ↗︎. For example the current header on the Plot documentation: The examples link in the Framework sidebar: The subdocs in the Documentation sidebar: This little arrow provides a hint to users that they are leaving the site and that the link will open in a new window. I violated this rule with the GitHub stars ★ partly as a technical shortcut and more importantly to avoid dual symbols ★↗︎ which felt busy. It was a little bit cheeky to replace the ↗︎ with a ★ to explain what the number meant — sacrificing a bit of utility (since it’s already pretty clear that a link to GitHub will take you off-site).
author: mbostock

body: That said, maybe the arrow isn’t needed because it’s similarly obvious that the release number is an off-site link…
author: mbostock

body: Can you point me to the docs for this? I’m curious.
author: mbostock

body:
I can't find the official documentation on the VitePress site for this, but [they do mention that](https://vitepress.dev/guide/markdown#external-links), for their Markdown extensions, 'Outbound links automatically get target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"'.

Looking at the code for VPButton [here](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/blob/d870ba48ea30aa496cfeb2198a21344047c5867b/src/client/theme-default/components/VPButton.vue#L36) and VPLink [here](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/blob/d870ba48ea30aa496cfeb2198a21344047c5867b/src/client/theme-default/components/VPLink.vue#L33) it does look like they default external links to have `rel="noreferrer"`.
author: CobusT

body: I see, so it’s just that we’re overriding the `rel` attribute to not have the default `noreferrer`. 👍
